title: Five-Year Early Childhood Mortality Rates
description: Neonatal, post-neonatal, infant, child, and under-5 mortality rates for the 5-year period preceding the survey, according to background characteristics: Philippines, 2022 and 2025.

chartData.title: Five-Year Early Childhood Mortality Rates
chartData.subtitle: Neonatal, post-neonatal, infant, child, and under-5 mortality rates by background characteristic, Philippines, 2022 and 2025.
notes: Childhood mortality rates are expressed as the number of deaths per 1,000 live births.
Neonatal mortality: Deaths within the first 28 days of life
Postneonatal mortality: The difference between infant and neonatal mortality
Infant mortality: Deaths before the first birthday
Child mortality: Deaths between the first and fifth birthday
Under-five mortality: Deaths before the fifth birthday
chartData.source: Philippine Statistics Authority, 2022 and 2025 National Demographic and Health Survey

insights.title: Insights on Five-Year Early Childhood Mortality Rates
insights.subtitle: Key patterns in neonatal, infant, child, and under-5 mortality by sex and residence.
insights.overview: Across the five-year period preceding the survey, mortality levels are lower in 2025 than in 2022 for all five indicators at the national total. The largest declines are in infant and under-5 mortality, while post-neonatal mortality changes only slightly.

sections.heading: National totals moved down across all indicators
sections.body: At the national level, each mortality indicator is lower in 2025 than in 2022. Neonatal mortality declined from 15 to 12, post-neonatal from 7 to 6, infant from 22 to 18, child from 5 to 3, and under-5 from 26 to 22. The broad pattern is improvement, not change in only one part of early-childhood risk.

sections.heading: Male-female differences remain, but at lower levels in 2025
sections.body: In both years, rates are generally higher among boys than girls, especially for infant and under-5 mortality. In 2025, under-5 mortality is 24 among males and 19 among females; in 2022 it is 29 and 23, respectively. The sex gap is still visible in 2025, but both groups are at lower levels than in 2022.

sections.heading: Urban-rural contrast changes by indicator
sections.body: Residence patterns are mixed rather than one-directional. In 2025, neonatal mortality is higher in urban areas (14) than rural (10), while post-neonatal and child mortality are higher in rural areas (7 and 4) than urban (5 and 2). Under-5 mortality is close (21 urban, 22 rural), showing that the combined total reflects offsetting urban-rural differences across components.

sections.heading: Post-neonatal mortality is the most stable measure
sections.body: Compared with the other indicators, post-neonatal mortality moves least across year and subgroup comparisons. Nationally it shifts from 7 to 6, and subgroup values stay within a narrow band. Most of the visible movement from 2022 to 2025 comes from neonatal, infant, child, and under-5 rates.

takeaways: For the five-year period preceding the survey, all national childhood mortality indicators are lower in 2025 than in 2022.
Male mortality remains higher than female mortality on most indicators, but both groups improved by 2025.
Urban-rural differences are not uniform; they vary by mortality component, with the overall under-5 gap now small.
